coredata.dc:title: NIR-emitting carbon dots for discriminative imaging and photo-inactivation of pathogenic bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Weijian
Gu, Hua
Liu, Wenkai
Lv, Chengyuan
Du, Jianjun
Fan, Jiangli
Peng, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: The rapid identification of pathogens and their viability are important for the effective diagnosis and treatment of infectious diseases. In this study, real near-infrared (NIR)-emitting carbon dots (Cy7-CDs, λmax = 710 nm) were synthesized using a solvothermal reaction method, in which the optical properties of cyanine dye were partially retained, and the water solubility and fluorescence quantum yield were greatly improved. The abundant functional groups on the surface of Cy7-CDs facilitated their interactions with biomolecules within the bacteria. Cy7-CDs quickly integrated into cell wall of G+ bacteria with obvious fluorescence or outer membrane of G- bacteria with no fluorescence. As a result, Cy7-CDs can not only be used to identify bacterial species (G+ or G- bacteria), but can also be used to quickly and efficiently monitor bacterial viability using fluorescence imaging. Furthermore, within 15 min, 97.7% Escherichia coli (G-) and 95.8% Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (G+) were killed rapidly by Cy7-CDs, due to its powerful ability to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) under single light irradiation (650 nm). Therefore, the Cy7-CDs strategy has the potential to be used for diagnosis and treatment of bacterial disease integration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NIR-emitting carbon dots
Discriminative imaging
Fluorescence imaging-guided photodynamic-inactivating bacteria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bacterial culture and discrimination imaging of Cy7-CDs
Staining Live/Dead bacteria performance of Cy7-CDs and commercial dyes.
Antibacterial activity of Cy7-CDs in vitro
Synthesis and characterization of NIR-emitting CDs (Cy7-CDs)
Comparison between Cy7-CDs and commercial dyes on bacterial viability assessment
Antibacterial activity of Cy7-CDs in vitro

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Imaging mechanism of Cy7-CDs in bacteria.

Characterization of Cy7-CDs. (A) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of Cy7-CDs. (B) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Cy7-CDs. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cy7-CDs and Cy-COOH. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cy7-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (E) Photoluminescence decay curves of the Cy7-CDs. (F) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the Cy7-CDs.
Visual fluorescence imaging and discrimination of live and dead bacteria (MRSA, G+ and E. Coli (ATCC8739), G-) by Cy7-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs and bacteria(G-) treating with Cy7-CDs (10 μg/mL), respectively. (B) Zeta potential of Cy7-CDs, live bacteria (G+ and G-) and bacteria with Cy7-CDs in PBS solution, respectively. (C) Fluorescence intensity of liposomal@Cy7-CDs in methanol and PBS solution, respectively. (D) Imaging mechanism of Cy7-CDs in bacteria.
Confocal fluorescence images of live and dead MRSA colocalized with Calcein AM (λex/λecm = 488 nm/514 nm)/PI (λex/λem = 561 nm/620 nm) and Cy7-CDs (λex/λem = 640 nm/710 nm), respectively.

(A) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of MRSA and E. Coli (ATCC8739) after treatment under different conditions (PBS, Cy7-CDs: 150 µg/mL, Cy7-CDs + Light (650 nm, 20 mW/cm2, 15 min)). (B) Detection of live bacteria and further killing them by light irradiation (650 nm, 20 mW/cm2, 15 min).

(A) Photographs of wounds of the mice after various treatments (B) Wound closure after various treatments (C) Changes in bodyweight after various treatments. (Group I: PBS, Group II: Cy7-CDs (160 μg/mL), Group III: Cy7-CDs + Light (650 nm, 20 mW/cm2), scale bar: 10 mm).

head.ce:title: NIR-emitting carbon dots for discriminative imaging and photo-inactivation of pathogenic bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology, Ningbo 315016, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid identification of pathogens and their viability are important for the effective diagnosis and treatment of infectious diseases. In this study, real near-infrared (NIR)-emitting carbon dots (Cy7-CDs, λmax = 710 nm) were synthesized using a solvothermal reaction method, in which the optical properties of cyanine dye were partially retained, and the water solubility and fluorescence quantum yield were greatly improved. The abundant functional groups on the surface of Cy7-CDs facilitated their interactions with biomolecules within the bacteria. Cy7-CDs quickly integrated into cell wall of G+ bacteria with obvious fluorescence or outer membrane of G- bacteria with no fluorescence. As a result, Cy7-CDs can not only be used to identify bacterial species (G+ or G- bacteria), but can also be used to quickly and efficiently monitor bacterial viability using fluorescence imaging. Furthermore, within 15 min, 97.7% Escherichia coli (G-) and 95.8% Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (G+) were killed rapidly by Cy7-CDs, due to its powerful ability to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) under single light irradiation (650 nm). Therefore, the Cy7-CDs strategy has the potential to be used for diagnosis and treatment of bacterial disease integration.

ce:section.ce:para: Clinically, infections associated with pathogenic bacteria or conditioned pathogens pose a serious threat to human health [1–7]. Bacteria may infect different parts of the body to cause different diseases, or similar diseases may be caused by different bacterial infections [8–10]. For example, Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacteria, G+ bacteria) can cause skin and soft tissue infection, or cause other infections, such as pneumonia, meningitis, and sepsis. Gram-negative bacteria (G- bacteria) (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Himophilus influenzae) can also cause similar clinical manifestations [7,9,11]. Most of the former is sensitive to penicillin, while the latter is insensitive to penicillin, but sensitive to streptomycin and chloramphenicol [12,13]. Although the Gram staining method is continually used as a historical gold standard, it is restricted to a complex staining process [14–16]. To effectively diagnose and treat infectious diseases, the development of new techniques for the rapid detection and identification of pathogens is desired [17].

Detection of bacterial viability is an indispensable index to identify the efficacy of drugs [16,18,19]. The conventional method is the standard plate count (SPC), which is time-consuming and labor intensive, and usually takes several hours or days to yield the results [20–22]. Other methods such as electron microscopy, field of view (FOV) diffraction, atomic force microscopy (AFM), mechanical trap surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (MTSERS), polymerase chain reaction (PCR), and synchrotron radiation infrared spectromicroscopy, have been developed to improve the detection efficiency [23–28]. However, these involve complicated operation practices and false positive results still exist. Thus, several fluorescent dyes (e.g., propidium iodide (PI)), propidium monoazide (PMA), calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein-AM), and green nucleic acid stain (SYTOX) have been designed to identify live/dead bacteria owing to their distinct advantages, such as in situ and real-time imaging capabilities, quick response times, and ease of operability [29–31].

Compared to organic dyes, however, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials (less than 10 nm in size), have received considerable attention because of their superior optical properties, ease of synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, anti-photobleaching effects, and outstanding water solubility [32–34]. The reported CDs are not only able to distinguish bacterial viability by staining dead cells, but have also been verified as a typical nano-photosensitizer for inactivation of bacteria, while bacteria do not easily develop drug resistance [14,18,35–40]. However, most of them utilize short wavelength light (<600 nm) as an excitation source, which could cause severe photodamage to tissues [37,40,41]. Some photo-responsive materials (e.g., metal–organic framework (MOF) heterojunction, black phosphorus nanosheets (BPs), chitosan/Ag/MoS2) have been developed to kill bacteria effectively by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) under long wavelength light irradiation (>600 nm) [42–44]. Thus, obtaining CDs with the ability to excite at long wavelengths is of vital importance for imaging or photodynamic inactivation of bacteria, as this may then avoid self-fluorescence by the target tissues and reduce phototoxicity [45–47]. Unfortunately, CDs with longer excitation wavelengths have low quantum yields (QYs) as well, which could limit their further application in biological imaging [45,46].

Cyanine 7 (Cy7) dyes possess a strong near-infrared (NIR) absorption character and can realize NIR fluorescence imaging under single-wavelength excitation [48]. However, they suffer from aggregation-caused quenching effects and undergo photobleaching during long-term imaging [36]. Herein, by introducing carboxyl groups to Cy7 dye to enrich the surface functional groups and PEG600 to improve the water solubility, real NIR-emitting CDs (Cy7-CDs, λmax = 710 nm) were synthesized according to the solvothermal reaction method [49,50]. When Cy7-COOH was prepared into Cy7-CDs, the optical properties of cyanine dye were partially retained, and the water solubility and fluorescence quantum yield were greatly improved. The abundant functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and benzene groups) on the surface of Cy7-CDs facilitated their interactions with biomolecules within bacteria. Accordingly, Cy7-CDs could not only be used to identify bacterial species (G+ or G- bacteria), but can also quickly and efficiently monitor bacterial viability through fluorescence imaging. Furthermore, Cy7-CDs could generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon single light irradiation (650 nm) to effectively kill them (Fig. 1 ). Therefore, the Cy7-CDs strategy achieved the purpose of diagnosis and treatment of bacterial disease integration.

Cy7-CDs were synthesized using a facile solvothermal method. In brief, Cy-COOH (50.0 mg) and PEG600 (500.0 mg) were initially dissolved in 10 mL methanol solution and subsequently added to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The reaction mixture was then transferred to an oven and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature (25 °C), violet suspensions were obtained. The resultant solution was then purified by silica column chromatography using a mixture of petroleum ether, methylene chloride, and methanol as eluents. Finally, the blue fraction eluted from the column was collected and dried under vacuum to obtain NIR-emitting CDs (Cy7-CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial culture and discrimination imaging of Cy7-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Gram-positive bacteria(G+), including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Staphylococcus aureus (SA), and Streptococcus agalactiae (GBS), were cultured in tryptic soy agar (TSA) medium. Gram-negative bacteria(G-), including Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA), Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC8379), Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC27853), and Acinetobacter baumannii (AB), were collectively cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium. After incubation in a shaking incubator (150 rpm, 37 °C, 18 h), fresh bacterial suspensions were obtained. Subsequently, bacterial suspensions were centrifuged (5000 rpm, 5 min) and washed twice with sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Before imaging by confocal fluorescence microscopy (CLSM), all live bacteria or dead bacteria (obtained by treatment at 121 °C for 1 h in a steam-heated vessel) were stained with Cy7-CDs (10 μg/mL) for 5 min in cell culture dishes. Using CLSM imaging, 100 × oil-immersion objectives were employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining Live/Dead bacteria performance of Cy7-CDs and commercial dyes.
ce:section.ce:para: The pretreated bacterial suspensions (live or dead bacteria) (1 mL, 108 cfu/mL) were stained with Calcein-AM (2 mmol/L, 1 µL) and PI (1.5 mmol/L, 2 µL) for 30 min in the dark. The suspensions were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min and washed several times with sterile PBS buffer. After shaking, the stained bacteria were stained with Cy7-CDs for 5 min. Finally, the colocalized images of the live/dead bacterial cell were observed using CLSM. Calcein AM (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–540 nm)/PI (λex = 561 nm, λem = 600–640 nm) and Cy7-CDs (λex = 640 nm, λem = 680–760 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of Cy7-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained bacterial suspensions (MRSA and E. Coli (ATCC8739)) were directly diluted to 1 × 106 cfu/mL. The suspensions (1 mL) were added into centrifugal tube (5 mL). Different doses of Cy7-CDs were added into the suspensions, resulting in final concentrations of Cy7-CDs of 0, 90 and 160 µg/mL, respectively. The suspensions were then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. At the same condition, the bacterial suspensions were further irradiated by 650 nm-light (20 mW/cm2, 15 min). Finally, the bacteria were placed on the solid LB agar plates by a spread plate method and cultured for 24 h at a constant-temperature incubator (37 °C) before counting the number of the bacteria colonies. Each concentration was prepared and measured in triplicate, and all experiments were repeated at least twice in parallel.

All animal procedures were carried out in accordance with institutional animal use and guidelines approved by the Dalian Medical University Animal Care and Use Committee [51]. The female BALB/c mice (8 weeks, 16–20 g) were purchased from Dalian Medical University and used to establish wound infection models. Briefly, we first fabricated a round wound (diameter: ca. 10.0 mm) on the back of the mouse, then 100 µL of the freshly grown MRSA (1.5 × 109 cfu/mL) was injected to the local wound. After 3 days, the mice were randomly divided into three groups with five mice in each group: (I) PBS, (II) Cy7-CDs (160 µg/mL), (III) Cy7-CDs + Light. Subsequently, 100 µL of PBS or Cy7-CDs solution was injected into the wound of the local cyst. After 30 min of application, for the light irradiation groups, the wound was exposed to a 650 nm light (20 mW/cm2) for 15 min. Photographing the wound diameter and measuring the body weight during the wound healing. After 13 days treatments, all the mice were sacrificed. All the major organs were excised for H&E staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of NIR-emitting CDs (Cy7-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The starting precursor, carboxyl cyanine dye (Cy-COOH), showed strong absorption and emission in the near-infrared region (λmax = 830 nm, Fig. S2A). Although Cy-COOH possesses two carboxyl groups, it still has poor solubility and low QYs [49]. Thus, polyethylene glycol (PEG600) was also used as a precursor, which is beneficial for improving the water solubility of the Cy7-CDs. The synthesis of Cy7-CDs involved the facile solvothermal treatment of the reaction precursors at 160 °C for 6 h, followed by purification via silica column chromatography (Fig. S1). The HR-TEM image shows that the obtained Cy7-CDs were uniform and well separated from each other, exhibiting a spherical-like structure with an average diameter of 3.4 nm (Fig. S2B). The Cy7-CDs are non-crystalline structures because lattice fringes are hardly observed in the HR-TEM images (Fig. 2 A). The XRD results shown in Fig. 2B further validate this result.

From the UV–visible absorption spectra of the Cy7-CDs (Fig. 2C), there are two main absorption peaks at 367 and 620 nm corresponding to the π–π* and n-π*transition of CC and CN/CO bonds of the aromatic rings, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence emission peak at 710 nm is independent of the excitation wavelength, and its optimal excitation wavelength is consistent with the maximum absorption wavelength (620 nm), thereby indicating that the surface structures and states were responsible for the near-infrared emission. The surface groups (OH, CO, CC, NH, etc.) on Cy7-CDs were further confirmed by FTIR (Fig. 2F) and XPS (Fig. S3). The lifetime curve can be fitted with a double exponential function with two lifetimes of 1.4 ns (80.9%) and 4.9 ns (19.1%), thereby demonstrating the presence of the different energy levels on the surface of Cy7-CDs (Fig. 2E) [35]. Thus, the corresponding luminescence mechanism may be attributed to the surface molecular state fluorescence, which is related to the surface groups on Cy7-CDs and shows excitation-independent fluorescence emission [52].

The absolute fluorescence quantum efficiency (Φf) of Cy7-CDs was 18.8%, which is 94 times that of Cy-COOH (Φf = 0.2%) in methanol. Thus, Cy7-CDs could be used as fluorescent probes with high-resolution imaging. In addition, up to 16.6% of singlet oxygen (1O2) quantum yield for Cy7-CDs was calculated using 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a probe and methylene blue (MB) as the reference, which was 5.2 times that of Cy-COOH (Fig. S4D). Therefore, the excitation-independent near-infrared fluorescence and the generation of 1O2 are beneficial for subsequent bacterial imaging and bacterial killing.

The cell structures and/or composition of G+ and G- bacteria are completely different. Usually, the G+ bacteria contain a single cell membrane surrounded by a thick layer of peptidoglycan, whereas the G- bacterial surface contains a thin layer of peptidoglycan sandwiched between two layers of phospholipids (Fig. 4D) [54]. To investigate the different staining of G+ and G- bacteria, the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs was initially detected after mixing with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (from E. coli (ATCC8739) bacteria, G-), total protein of E. coli (ATCC8739, G-), and liposome (mimic bacterial outer membrane), respectively (Fig. S8) [55]. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs changed slightly after mixing with LPS, while the total protein of E. coli (ATCC8739, G-) significantly reduced the fluorescence signal. This result suggests that the LPS (negative charge) did not interact with negatively charged Cy7-CDs, while the abundant functional groups on the surface of Cy7-CDs facilitated their interactions with the protein. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of Cy7-CDs was completely quenched by encapsulation in liposome vesicles (Fig. 4C), and this indicates that the Cy7-CDs were locked in the G- bacterial outer membrane and were unable to enter the cell wall, thus resulting in a very weak fluorescence signal (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between Cy7-CDs and commercial dyes on bacterial viability assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the staining performance of Cy7-CDs on both live and dead bacteria, a mixture of live and dead MRSA (G+) was co-incubated with Cy7-CDs and the commercial dyes Calcein AM and PI, as Calcein AM can stain live cells with green fluorescence, while PI can stain dead cells to produce red fluorescence. As shown in Figs. 5 , S9, 10 and 11, Cy7-CDs and PI stain dead bacteria (G+) with red and blue fluorescence (red and blue are pseudo color), respectively. Strong purple signals were observed in the merged images. Simultaneously, all of the cell walls of live bacteria (G+) could be stained by Cy7-CDs, showing strong red fluorescence signals. In comparison, lower live G+ bacteria staining efficiency was observed in the presence of Calcein AM with green fluorescence (Figs. 5, S9 and 10). In addition, the excitation wavelengths were 488 and 561 nm, and the corresponding emissions were collected in the range of 500–540 nm (Calcein AM) and 600–640 nm (PI), respectively. Significantly, Cy7-CDs, as a fluorescent probe using a single-excitation wavelength (λex = 640 nm), not only minimized the complexity of fluorescence detection, but also simultaneously distinguished live/dead bacteria in the same channel (λem = 680–760 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of Cy7-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the exceptional 1O2 generation ability of Cy7-CDs, we subsequently evaluated its antibacterial efficacy against MRSA(G+) and E. coli (ATCC8739, G-) in vitro. The total ROS within bacteria were first detected by the fluorescent probe 2,7-dichlorodifluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), which could emit green fluorescence in the presence of ROS [51]. As shown in Fig. S12, after light exposure, the bacteria displayed clear green fluorescence signals, indicating that Cy7-CDs can rapidly generate ROS and has the potential to act as an ideal photosensitive agent for bacteria killing. Then, SEM was used to observe cell morphology changes in MRSA(G+) and E. coli cells (ATCC8739, G-). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the morphology of bacteria in the control groups (PBS or Cy7-CDs) remained unchanged with unbroken surfaces, thereby indicating that the obtained Cy7-CDs have good biocompatibility. In contrast, the cell surfaces became damaged and wrinkled with the disappearance of the original shape after light irradiation. To accurately investigate the antibacterial effect of Cy7-CDs, an in vitro antibacterial experiment against MRSA(G+) and E. coli (ATCC8739, G-) was tested by standard plate counting assays (Fig. S13). Notably, when the concentration of Cy7-CDs group reached 160 μg/mL, under irradiation with 650-nm light, the bacterial survival decreased from 100% to 2.3% (E. coli (ATCC8739), G-) and to 5.2% (MRSA, G+) (Fig. S14). In addition, when the live bacteria were co-incubated with Cy7-CDs, the cell walls of G+ bacteria were low, whereas no fluorescence signal was found in G- bacteria under confocal laser scanning microscopy (Fig. 6B). Subsequently, both the labeled bacteria were effectively inactivated upon 650 nm-light and exhibited strong red fluorescence signals, realizing visual bacterial detection, which corresponded to the results of discrimination imaging (Fig. 6B and S15). Thus, Cy7-CDs have great application potential in the diagnosis of bacterial infections and the treatment of bacterial diseases.
Inspired by the antibacterial activity of Cy7-CDs in vitro, we employed antibacterial tests in vivo using a mouse model with MRSA-infected skin wounds.

The wound healing process at different time is illustrated as Fig. 7 A. All the wounds exhibited severe infection with abscess on the 3rd day. Then the Cy7-CDs were applied to the infected abscess wounds (group II, III) and then exposed (group III) to 650 nm light (20 W/cm2, 15 min). At the 5th day, groups I and II still hold obvious abscess at the wounds, while group III demonstrates to form the scab. Clearly, the wound healing rate in group III was much faster than that of control groups I, II (Fig. 7B). Finally, group III displayed complete recovery than the control groups I and II on the 13th day. At the same time, the body weights of mice showed negligible changes during the wound healing process (Fig. 7C), and no significant inflammation in any major organ was observed (Fig. S16) in hematoxylin-and-eosin staining, which suggested that Cy7-CDs has excellent biosafety in vivo.

In this study, Cy7-CDs (∼3.4 nm) were formed by a solvothermal reaction using carboxyl cyanine dyes and PEG600 as raw materials, and showed a strong absorption in the near-infrared region. When cyanine dye (Cy-COOH) was prepared into Cy7-CDs, the photostability and fluorescence quantum yield greatly improved. In particular, Cy7-CDs could generate singlet oxygen (1O2) under 650 nm light irradiation as a nano-photosensitizer. The abundant functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and benzene groups) on the surface of Cy7-CDs enabled them to rapidly and effectively bind to bacteria, causing low background fluorescence in PBS buffer, which could achieve high-performance wash-free bacteria imaging. Thus, Cy7-CDs can not only be used to discriminate between G+ and G- bacteria and monitor their respective life and death, but can also be used to realize fluorescence imaging-guided photodynamic anti-bacteria. The present results are expected to provide a new strategy for the synthesis of multifunctional CDs for the diagnosis and treatment of bacterial diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (21878039, 21925802, 22090010), National Key Research and Development Plan (2018AAA0100301), and the NSFC-Liaoning United Fund (U1908202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.137384.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoexcited quantum dots for killing multidrug-resistant bacteria
